g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Distributed System Models and Enabling Technologies - the foundation of cloud computing.
g.text: Distributed System Models and Enabling Technologies

g.data-narration: A distributed system is a collection of independent computers that appears as a single coherent system to its users.
g.text: Collection of independent computers appearing as single coherent system

g.data-narration: Cloud computing is not revolutionary - it evolved from distributed systems. Understanding this evolution is key to mastering cloud technologies.
g.text: Cloud Computing is an EVOLUTION of distributed systems, not a revolution

g.data-narration: A cluster is a group of tightly connected computers working together as a single system, focused on high performance and high availability.
g.text: Group of loosely or tightly connected computers viewed as single system

g.data-narration: Clusters use homogeneous systems, centralized management via master node, and tight coupling with high-speed interconnects like InfiniBand.
g.text: Identical hardware and software for simplicity
Example: University HPC lab with 100 servers for scientific simulations

g.data-narration: A computational grid federates resources from multiple organizations to solve large problems, spanning geographic locations.
g.text: Federation of resources from multiple domains for large-scale problem solving

g.data-narration: Grids embrace heterogeneous systems, decentralized management where each organization controls its own resources, and loose coupling over WAN.
g.text: Example: LHC Computing Grid (WLCG) - processes petabytes from particle collisions

g.data-narration: Cloud provides on-demand, scalable computing resources as a utility service. Its evolution combines best of clusters and grids.
g.text: On-demand, scalable computing resources delivered as a utility service

g.data-narration: Key cloud characteristics: virtualization for resource abstraction, elasticity for automatic scaling, and service-oriented delivery models.
g.text: Example: AWS EC2, S3, RDS - rent virtual servers and pay only for usage

g.data-narration: Virtualization is the cornerstone of cloud computing. Hypervisors create an abstraction layer, running multiple VMs on a single physical machine.
g.text: Core technology - hypervisor creates abstraction layer for multiple isolated VMs

g.data-narration: Service-Oriented Architecture enables different systems to communicate via standardized web services using HTTP, XML, and JSON.
g.text: ENABLING TECH 2: SOA AND WEB SERVICES
Standardized services enabling communication - foundation for Everything-as-a-Service (XaaS)

g.data-narration: Utility computing provides metered services like electricity. Autonomic computing enables self-management - auto-scaling and self-healing.
g.text: ENABLING TECH 3: UTILITY AND AUTONOMIC COMPUTING
Examples: AWS Auto Scaling, Google Cloud Autoscaling, Utility billing models

g.data-narration: Comparing the three models: clusters for performance, grids for sharing, clouds for elasticity - each serves different purposes.
g.text: COMPARISON: Cluster vs Grid vs Cloud

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: Distributed systems form cloud foundation. Clusters, grids, and clouds each have distinct characteristics and use cases.
The evolution from clusters to grids to clouds represents a shift towards greater flexibility, scalability, and accessibility of computing resources.
g.text: Cloud computing evolved from distributed systems fundamentals
